Translate Póster soviético conmemorativo de San Jorge de 1945.

San Jorge a caballo, representado como un soldado soviético, atraviesa a un dragón tricéfalo, cuyas cabezas forman una esvástica.

Soviet commemorative poster from 1945 of Saint George.

Saint George on horseback, depicted as a Soviet soldier, spears a three-headed dragon, whose heads form a swastika.

Translate 鉄道文化むら周辺はとてもロケーションがよく、めがね橋まで遠征しちゃいました。
＊安中市にロケ確認済

The area around the Railway Culture Village has a very nice location, and we even went on an expedition to the Megane Bridge.
* Location scouting completed in Annaka City

Translate 天高くひろがる勇気❗
アゲてひろがるワンダホー❗

最強のコンビ🐥🦋❗

Courage spreading high and wide❗
Lift it up, spread the wonder❗

The strongest duo🐥🦋❗

Yes, human blood does oxidize. The process of oxidation in human blood is a crucial part of how our bodies use oxygen. When red blood cells travel through the lungs, hemoglobin molecules within these cells bind to oxygen molecules. This process, facilitated by the iron in hemoglobin, essentially involves the transfer of electrons between oxygen and iron, fitting the chemical definition of oxidation.

The hemoglobin that has bound oxygen (oxyhemoglobin) is then transported throughout the body via the bloodstream, delivering oxygen to various tissues. Once the oxygen is released into the tissues, the hemoglobin becomes deoxygenated and returns to the lungs to pick up more oxygen, repeating the cycle.

The change in the oxidation state of the iron within hemoglobin is what causes the change in color of blood. Oxygenated blood is bright red, while deoxygenated blood is darker. This color change is due to the oxidative state of the iron as it binds with oxygen.
What is the half-life of Iron-56?

Iron-56 (^56Fe) is a stable isotope of iron and does not have a half-life since it does not undergo radioactive decay under normal conditions. Being stable means that ^56Fe does not change into another element over time, which is characteristic of radioactive isotopes that do have half-lives due to their decay processes. Iron-56 is one of the most common isotopes of iron found in nature.
